title: Bicarbonate scouring paste
subtitle: A mild abrasive cleaning paste with liquid castile soap and lemon
excerpt: A gentle scouring paste made from bicarbonate of soda and liquid castile soap with optional lemon essential oil. Effective on sinks, bath surrounds, hob rings, and ceramic tiles. Mildly abrasive without scratching; the castile soap suspends the bicarbonate and lifts grease simultaneously.
sourceNotes: UK Health Security Agency, Guidance on household cleaning products (2023). Castile soap dilution guidance, Dr. Bronner's usage guide (drbronner.com, May 2026).

content.text: A bicarbonate scouring paste. Bicarbonate of soda is a mild alkali and a mild scrubber. It is fine enough not to scratch enamel, ceramic, or stainless steel. It is still gritty enough to lift limescale and dried food. Liquid castile soap holds the bicarbonate in the paste. It also helps the paste cling to upright surfaces and cuts through grease. The two are fine together. Most 'do not mix' warnings are about castile soap with vinegar, which cancels both out.

This paste is not a disinfectant. It removes surface grease and limescale; for high-touch surfaces requiring disinfection, follow with the all-purpose surface spray or a dedicated disinfectant. Not suitable for natural stone (marble, granite, limestone); the abrasive will scratch polished surfaces over time.

items.name: Bicarbonate of soda
items.prepNote: fine bicarbonate of soda (baking soda). The abrasive and deodorising base.

items.name: Castile liquid soap
items.prepNote: concentrated liquid castile soap. Binds the bicarbonate into a paste and provides degreasing action. Add more or less to reach a thick, spreadable consistency.

items.name: Lemon essential oil
items.prepNote: Citrus limon lemon essential oil. Approximately 50 drops. Adds a bright fragrance and a supplementary degreasing note. Omit for an unscented paste.

content.text: Measure the bicarbonate into a wide-mouthed jar or bowl. Pour the castile soap over the bicarbonate and stir with the silicone spatula. The mixture will fizz gently as the soap (slightly alkaline) contacts the bicarbonate; this is normal and stops quickly. Add the essential oil if using and stir through. The paste should be thick and spreadable but not crumbly. If too stiff, add castile soap a teaspoon at a time; if too wet, add a little more bicarbonate. Transfer to a sealed glass jar.

Scoop a teaspoon of paste onto a damp cloth or sponge and apply to the surface in circular motions. For baked-on hob residue or limescale ring in a sink, leave the paste for 5 minutes before scrubbing. Rinse thoroughly with water. For bath and basin surrounds, apply paste with an old toothbrush around taps and grout lines.

Safe on: stainless steel sinks, enamel baths, cool ceramic hob rings, sealed ceramic tiles, porcelain. Not safe on: natural stone (marble, granite, slate, limestone), where the grit scratches the polish. Not safe on aluminium either, which bicarbonate slowly eats. And not safe on non-stick pans, where any scrub takes off the coating.

2 weeks in a sealed glass jar. The paste contains water from the castile soap; make small batches and use quickly. If the paste dries out in the jar, add a few drops of castile soap and stir to re-loosen. Discard if it smells sour or rancid.
